engineGuide.h2: Mercedes Benz C Class Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Mercedes Benz C Class across all generations, with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 2.2L Diesel
entries.history: Mercedes‘ first common-rail diesel for the C-Class, introduced on the W203. Renowned for longevity when maintained but plagued by injector seal failures.
compatibleVariants: C220 CDI (W203), C220 CDI Classic, C220 CDI Elegance, C220 CDI Avantgarde
commonFailures: Injector seal leaks causing black death carbon buildup (50,000-80,000 miles)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM611 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.2L Diesel
entries.history: The OM646 Evo was a refinement of the OM611, addressing injector sealing and adding piezo injectors for cleaner combustion and better refinement across the W203 and early W204 ranges.
compatibleVariants: C200 CDI (W203), C220 CDI (W203), C220 CDI Classic, C220 CDI Elegance, C220 CDI Avantgarde, C220 CDI Sport
commonFailures: Injector seal leaks (improved but still reported)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM646 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.1L Diesel
entries.history: The OM651 was the backbone of Mercedes diesel sales for over a decade, fitted to millions of C-Class, E-Class, and Sprinter vans. Powerful and efficient but notorious for timing chain failure due to the chain being mounted at the rear of the engine .
compatibleVariants: C200 CDI (W204), C220 CDI (W204), C250 CDI (W204), C220 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY, C220 CDI Sport, C250 CDI Sport, C220d (W205), C250d (W205), C220 BlueTEC, C250 BlueTEC
commonFailures: Timing chain stretch and failure (60,000-100,000 miles)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM651 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 3.0L V6 Diesel
entries.history: Mercedes‘ 3.0-litre V6 diesel found in the C350 CDI and various models across the range. Smooth and torquey but known for oil cooler leaks that can lead to serious engine damage if ignored .
compatibleVariants: C350 CDI (W204), C350 CDI Sport, C350 CDI AMG Sport
commonFailures: Oil cooler seal leak (requires intake manifold removal, 80,000+ miles)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM642 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel
entries.history: Mercedes‘ all-new modular diesel engine family introduced in the W205 facelift. Steel pistons, stepped combustion bowls, and a 48V electrical architecture make it cleaner and quieter than the OM651 it replaced, with far fewer catastrophic failures .
commonFailures: DPF regeneration issues on short journeys
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM654 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol
entries.history: The M111 four-cylinder was the workhorse petrol engine of the W202 C-Class, known as the C180 and C200. Supercharger ("Kompressor") versions arrived later in the W203.
entries.cta: Get quotes for M111 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.6L / 1.8L Petrol
entries.history: The M271 brought supercharging ("Kompressor") and later CGI direct injection to the C-Class. Responsive but notorious for timing chain and camshaft adjuster issues, a major replacement driver in the used market .
compatibleVariants: C180 Kompressor (W203), C200 Kompressor (W203), C200 CGI (W204), C180 Kompressor (W204)
commonFailures: Timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster failure (50,000-80,000 miles)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M271 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol
entries.history: Mercedes‘ first turbocharged 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol. The M270 (transverse) and M274 (longitudinal) shared architecture, bringing modern performance and efficiency to the C-Class range.
commonFailures: Piston wrist pin failure (NHTSA investigation opened for M274 variants)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M270 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol
entries.history: The longitudinal version of Mercedes‘ 2.0-litre turbo four. Used across the W205 C-Class range from C200 to C300. Subject to an NHTSA investigation over cracked pistons linked to wrist pin failure .
commonFailures: Piston cracking (wrist pin / piston seizure, 40,000-80,000 miles)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M274 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.5L / 3.0L / 3.5L V6 Petrol
entries.history: Mercedes‘ V6 petrol family, with the M272 being an earlier 24-valve design and the M276 introducing direct injection and a 60-degree vee angle for smoother operation. Fitted to higher-spec C-Class models.
commonFailures: Balance shaft sprocket wear (M272, early production)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M276 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.5L / 2.0L MHEV Petrol
entries.history: Mercedes‘ newest four-cylinder petrol featuring a 48V integrated starter-generator (ISG) and electric auxiliary compressor. Used in the W206 C-Class with mild hybrid technology for improved efficiency and response .
commonFailures: Oil consumption (some early reports on M254 variants)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M254 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 6.2L V8 Petrol
entries.history: Mercedes-AMG‘s first independently developed V8 engine. Naturally aspirated, producing up to 520hp in the C63 AMG. Highly revered but with well-documented head bolt weakness in early production units .
compatibleVariants: C63 AMG (W204), C63 AMG Performance Package
entries.cta: Get quotes for M156 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

entries.title: 4.0L V8 Petrol
entries.history: AMG‘s 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8, replacing the M156 in the W205 C63 AMG. Two turbochargers nestled in the vee ("hot inside V") for reduced lag and improved packaging.

commonProblems.h2: Mercedes Benz C Class Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.group: Mercedes Benz C Class OM651 Timing Chain Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz C Class OM651 Timing Chain Failure - Why Diesel Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz C Class C200 CDI, C220 CDI, C250 CDI (W204, 2008-2014, OM651); C220d, C250d (W205, 2014-2018, OM651)
problems.rootCause: The OM651 uses a timing chain mounted at the rear of the engine, adjacent to the gearbox. The chain stretches over time due to inadequate tensioner design and extended oil change intervals, eventually skipping teeth on the camshaft sprockets.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Preventative Chain Replacement (early rattle only)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replacement of timing chain, tensioners, guides, and sprockets (gearbox remains fitted, requires specialist tooling)
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000 - 80,000 miles; viable only if caught at first rattle before chain has jumped timing

repairOptions.tier: Major - Post-Failure Rebuild (skipped timing, bent valves)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Cylinder head removal, valve replacement, piston inspection, full timing component replacement, oil system flush
repairOptions.longevity: 40,000 - 60,000 miles; high labour cost makes specialist rebuild marginal vs replacement

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete replacement with professionally rebuilt OM651 unit featuring upgraded timing components, new seals, bench tested
repairOptions.longevity: 80,000+ miles expected; best long-term value on higher-mileage cars

problems.recommendation: On a C Class worth £3,000-£6,000, the preventative timing chain replacement at an independent specialist (£700-£1,100) is the clear choice if your car rattles on cold start but hasn't skipped timing. Once the chain has jumped, the labour to rebuild the head pushes costs towards replacement territory. For high-mileage cars (120,000+ miles) with a failed engine, a rebuilt unit with upgraded timing components is more reliable than attempting a repair.
problems.cta: Compare OM651 rebuilt engine prices for your Mercedes Benz C Class

problems.group: Mercedes Benz C Class M271 Timing Chain & Camshaft Adjuster Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz C Class M271 Timing Chain & Camshaft Adjuster Failure - Kompressor Engine Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz C Class C180 Kompressor, C200 Kompressor (W203, 2002-2007, M271); C180 Kompressor (W204, 2007-2010, M271)
problems.rootCause: The M271 features a single timing chain that stretches prematurely. Combined with failing camshaft adjuster sprockets (the "cam gear" wears internally), the engine loses timing, leading to rough running, misfires, and eventually valve-to-piston contact.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replacement of timing chain, both camshaft adjusters (sprockets), tensioner, and guides - gearbox remains fitted
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000 - 80,000 miles; highly effective if addressed before adjuster teeth shear

repairOptions.tier: Intermediate - Cylinder Head Rebuild (after timing slip, no piston damage)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Cylinder head removal, valve replacement, lapping seats, plus full timing components and adjusters
repairOptions.longevity: 40,000 - 60,000 miles; specialist pricing makes this viable for lower-mileage examples

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete replacement with professionally rebuilt M271 unit featuring upgraded timing components and new adjusters
repairOptions.longevity: 80,000+ miles expected; best option when piston or bore damage confirmed

problems.recommendation: The M271's camshaft adjuster failure often gives warning signs - a rattle on start-up and rough idling. Catch it here, and the £650-£950 specialist fix is excellent value. If you hear chain slap or the engine runs poorly under load, stop driving immediately. On a W203 C Class worth £1,500-£3,000, replacement rather than a head rebuild often makes more financial sense.
problems.cta: Get quotes for M271 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

problems.group: Mercedes Benz C Class M274 Piston Crack
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz C Class M274 Piston Crack - The 2.0 Litre Petrol Failure
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz C Class C200, C250, C300 (W205, 2014-2018, M274)
problems.rootCause: The M274 engine suffers from a wrist pin / piston seizure failure. The pin that connects the piston to the connecting rod seizes within the piston boss, causing the piston skirt to crack and cylinder bore scoring. An NHTSA investigation was opened into this failure across Mercedes M274-equipped vehicles.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: There is no known preventative repair for the M274 wrist pin fault - it is a design/manufacturing defect that manifests without prior warning signs

repairOptions.tier: Major - Single Piston Replacement (early detection, no bore damage)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Single piston and wrist pin replacement, cylinder hone, new rings, cylinder head gasket replacement, oil system flush
repairOptions.longevity: 40,000 - 60,000 miles; viable only if compression loss caught immediately

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine (bore scoring or multiple piston failure)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete replacement with professionally rebuilt M274 unit featuring upgraded pistons designed to address the wrist pin design flaw
repairOptions.longevity: 80,000+ miles expected; almost always the better financial decision on a W205

problems.recommendation: The M274 piston crack is the most frustrating failure on this list because there is no preventative fix. The first warning is often a misfire code and rough running. On a 2014-2018 W205 C Class worth £6,000-£12,000, a specialist replacement with an upgraded rebuilt unit (£3,200-£5,000 fitted) is almost always better value than attempting to rebuild the original engine. Ensure any replacement engine specifically states it has upgraded wrist pin/piston components.
problems.cta: Compare M274 rebuilt engine prices for your Mercedes Benz C Class

problems.group: Mercedes Benz C Class OM642 Oil Cooler Leak
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz C Class OM642 Oil Cooler Leak - The V6 Diesel Failure
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz C Class C350 CDI (W204, 2009-2014, OM642)
problems.rootCause: The OM642's oil cooler seal, located deep within the Vee of the engine, degrades over time, causing oil to leak externally and contaminate the wiring loom. If ignored, oil level drops critically, leading to turbo failure or bottom-end bearing seizure.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Oil Cooler Seal Replacement (leak detected early)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replacement of oil cooler seals, intake manifold removal, oil flush, new oil and filter, replacement of oil-soaked wiring loom sections
repairOptions.longevity: 80,000 - 100,000 miles; highly effective preventative job if caught at first sign of oil leak

repairOptions.tier: Intermediate - Seal Replacement + Turbo Inspection (oil level low, turbo contamination)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Oil cooler seals plus turbocharger inspection, oil feed line cleaning or replacement, full oil system flush, new wiring loom
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000 - 80,000 miles; specialist approach delivers significant saving vs dealer

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine (bearing damage or turbo failure)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete replacement with professionally rebuilt OM642 unit featuring new oil cooler seals, upgraded loom, and rebuilt or new turbocharger
repairOptions.longevity: 80,000+ miles expected; once bearings are damaged, rebuild is no longer viable

problems.recommendation: The OM642 oil cooler leak is a well-documented and predictable failure. Check your W204 C350 CDI's driveway for oil spots and inspect the loom near the ECU for oil wicking. Catch it early, and an £800-£1,200 specialist seal replacement is straightforward. On a C Class worth £4,000-£7,000, if the engine has already suffered oil starvation damage, replacement is the only safe route.
problems.cta: Get quotes for OM642 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

problems.group: Mercedes Benz C Class M156 Head Bolt Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz C Class M156 Head Bolt Failure - The C63 AMG Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz C Class C63 AMG (W204, pre-facelift, 2007-2011, engine numbers below #060658, M156)
problems.rootCause: Early M156 engines used defective head bolts with inadequate corrosion resistance. The bolts stretch or snap over time, particularly around cylinders 5-7, allowing coolant to enter the combustion chamber and causing coolant loss, misfires, and eventual head gasket failure.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Preventative Head Bolt Replacement (no gasket failure yet)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replacement of all head bolts with updated OEM-spec bolts, one-by-one without removing cylinder heads, camshaft locking required
repairOptions.longevity: Permanent fix; viable on any pre-facelift M156 regardless of mileage - excellent preventative value

repairOptions.tier: Major - Head Gasket Replacement (after bolt failure, coolant ingress)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Both cylinder heads removed, head gasket replacement, new updated head bolts, cylinder head skim and pressure test
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000+ miles expected; significantly higher cost due to head removal and gasket replacement

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine (bore wash or hydro-lock damage)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete replacement with professionally rebuilt M156 unit featuring updated head bolts, new timing components, and full bench test
repairOptions.longevity: Permanent solution; the only option if coolant has hydrolocked and bent a rod

problems.recommendation: If you own a pre-facelift W204 C63 AMG, consider preventative head bolt replacement as essential maintenance. The £1,600-£2,400 specialist cost is trivial compared to a £6,500-£11,000 engine replacement. Check your engine number - any M156 below #060658 is affected. On a C63 worth £15,000-£30,000, this is not optional - it is insurance.
problems.cta: Compare M156 rebuilt engine prices for your Mercedes Benz C63 AMG

problems.group: Mercedes Benz C Class OM651 Injector Seal Leak
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz C Class OM651 Injector Seal Leak - The Black Death
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz C Class C200 CDI, C220 CDI, C250 CDI (W204, 2008-2014, OM651); C220d, C250d (W205, 2014-2018, OM651)
problems.rootCause: The copper washer seal between the injector and cylinder head degrades over time, allowing combustion gases to escape past the seal. The escaping gas mixes with carbon and unburnt fuel, creating a hard, black, crusty deposit ("black death") around the injector that can seize the injector into the cylinder head.

repairOptions.tier: Minor - Single Injector Seal Replacement (caught early)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Removal of one injector, cleaning of seat, replacement of copper washer and clamp bolt, injector leak-off test
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000 - 80,000 miles; effective if addressed at first sign of chuffing noise or smell

repairOptions.tier: Intermediate - All 4 Injector Seals + Injector Extraction (moderate carbon buildup)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: All four injector seals replaced, carbon removal from bores, potential injector extraction tool required (if seized), leak-off testing of all injectors

repairOptions.tier: Full - Injector Replacement + Head Rebuild (injector seized, bore damaged)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Seized injector drilling/extraction, cylinder head removal, injector bore machining or helicoil, new injectors (one or more), full head rebuild
repairOptions.longevity: 40,000 - 60,000 miles; only viable if injector cannot be extracted non-destructively

problems.recommendation: The "black death" is insidious but slow-moving. If you hear a ticking or chuffing noise from the engine bay that changes with engine speed, or smell diesel fumes in the cabin, inspect the injector area immediately. Caught early, an £180-£300 single seal replacement is cheap peace of mind. On an older W204 C Class with 120,000+ miles where an injector has seized, replacement of the whole engine sometimes makes more sense than a £1,200-£2,200 head repair, depending on the car's value.
problems.cta: Get quotes for OM651 Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement

engineSizes.h2: Mercedes Benz C Class Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Mercedes Benz C Class across all UK generations is listed below, with compatible engine codes, model years, power outputs, and common failure points to help you identify your exact unit.

groups.title: Mercedes Benz C Class Diesel Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6-litre diesel, badged as the C180d, appeared in the W205 generation as an entry-level oil-burner using the OM626 engine. This Renault-sourced unit (Type R9M) was introduced to improve fuel economy and CO2 emissions, making the C-Class more accessible to fleet and economy-focused buyers. While economical, it lacks the refinement of Mercedes' larger in-house diesel designs.
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz C Class 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre diesel arrived with the OM654 in the facelift W205 and became the mainstay of the W206 generation, replacing the venerable 2.1-litre OM651. This all-aluminium, stepped-combustion-bowl design features steel pistons and 48V mild hybrid assistance on later units. It delivers significantly reduced friction and improved thermal efficiency, but early examples suffered from AdBlue system issues.
commonFailurePoints: DPF regeneration problems on short journeys
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz C Class 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 2.1L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.1-litre OM651 is the most common diesel fitted to the W204 and early W205 C-Class, powering the C200 CDI, C220 CDI, and C250 CDI across hundreds of thousands of UK cars. This four-cylinder common-rail unit became famous for its torque delivery and fuel economy, but equally infamous for rear-mounted timing chain failure and injector seal leaks ("black death"). It remains the highest-volume replacement engine for the C-Class in the UK independent specialist market.
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz C Class 2.1L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2-litre OM646 and its OM611 predecessor powered the W202, W203, and very early W204 C-Class models. These engines established Mercedes' reputation for long-lived diesel engineering, with many units exceeding 200,000 miles when maintained. The OM646 Evo introduced piezo injectors and improved refinement, but injector seal leaks remained a persistent issue throughout production.
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz C Class 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre OM642 V6 diesel was reserved for the C350 CDI in the W204 generation, offering a smooth, torquey alternative to the four-cylinder range. This 24-valve, variable-geometry turbo unit delivers effortless performance but is known for a labour-intensive oil cooler leak that requires intake manifold removal. The OM642 was Mercedes' workhorse V6 diesel across multiple model lines, and in the C-Class, it remains a specialist proposition due to its complexity and higher replacement costs.
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz C Class 3.0L diesel engines

groups.title: Mercedes Benz C Class Petrol Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre M264 petrol arrived with the W206 generation as a mild hybrid featuring a 48V integrated starter-generator (ISG). Badged as the C180 and C200, this unit replaced larger four-cylinders in the name of emissions reduction. The EQ Boost system allows coasting and electric assistance, but early examples have shown sensitivity to oil consumption and 48V battery reliability.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6-litre M271 was introduced in the W204 facelift as the C180 Kompressor, offering supercharged performance in a smaller displacement to reduce tax and emissions. The M271 used a belt-driven supercharger with a magnetic clutch to improve fuel economy when boost was not required. While responsive, the timing chain and camshaft adjuster issues that plagued the larger 1.8-litre version carried over to this smaller variant.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8-litre M271 was the mainstay petrol engine of the W203 and early W204 C-Class, offered in naturally aspirated and supercharged ("Kompressor") forms, as well as later CGI direct-injection versions. This unit was known for its eager response and competitive power outputs, but its reputation was damaged by timing chain and camshaft adjuster failures that often required complete engine replacement when neglected.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre M274 turbocharged four-cylinder replaced the M271 across the W205 generation, powering the C200, C250, and C300 with significantly improved torque and refinement. This engine introduced direct injection and a twin-scroll turbocharger. However, the M274 gained notoriety for piston cracking linked to wrist pin seizure, which prompted an NHTSA investigation and remains the primary driver of replacement demand for W205 petrol models.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5-litre M272 V6 was a rare petrol offering in the W203 C230, positioned between the four-cylinder and larger V6 options. This 24-valve unit featured variable intake manifold and four-valve-per-cylinder architecture. It offered smooth power delivery but was eventually phased out in favour of the 3.0-litre and turbocharged four-cylinder options, making it less common in today's replacement market.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 2.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre M276 V6 appeared in the W204 C300 and later the W205 C300 as a naturally aspirated and then direct-injection unit. This 60-degree V6 was smoother and more efficient than the M272 it replaced, featuring spray-guided direct injection and lightweight construction. While reliable compared to the four-cylinder M274, it is not immune to timing chain and high-pressure fuel pump issues.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 3.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.5-litre M272 and later M276 V6 powered the C350 in both the W204 and W205 generations, offering a genuinely sporting petrol option before the C43 AMG arrived. The later M276 version featured direct injection and a more aggressive cam profile. This displacement remains sought after by enthusiasts who prefer naturally aspirated response over turbocharged four-cylinders.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 3.5L petrol engine prices

groups.title: Mercedes Benz C Class Performance & Hybrid Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 2.0L Plug-In Hybrid Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre M274 plug-in hybrid, badged as the C350e, combined the standard turbocharged four-cylinder petrol with a 60kW electric motor and a 6.4kWh lithium-ion battery pack. This was Mercedes' first mainstream plug-in hybrid C-Class, offering electric-only range for urban driving. The complexity of the hybrid system means replacements are significantly more expensive than standard petrol units, and battery degradation is a growing concern on older examples.
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes Benz C Class 2.0L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 4.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.0-litre M177 twin-turbo V8 arrived with the W205 C63 AMG and C63 S AMG, replacing the naturally aspirated M156. This "hot inside V" design places the turbochargers between the cylinder banks for reduced lag and improved packaging. The M177 produces prodigious power but demands meticulous maintenance, and cylinder scoring has emerged as a concern on higher-mileage examples.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 4.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz C Class 6.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 6.2-litre M156 V8 was AMG's first independently developed engine, powering the W204 C63 AMG to legendary status. This naturally aspirated, high-revving unit produced up to 520hp in later variants and is widely regarded as one of the greatest AMG engines ever built. However, early production engines suffer from defective head bolts that can lead to catastrophic failure, making preventative replacement essential for any pre-facelift C63 owner.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz C Class 6.2L petrol engine prices

faq.h2: Mercedes Benz C Class Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does a Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement engine for a Mercedes C Class typically costs between £1,400 and £4,500 for a reconditioned unit, plus £500-£1,000 for fitting. Final price depends heavily on which engine code your C Class has.
keyPoints: OM651 diesel engines: £1,400-£3,500 for rebuilt unit
M274 petrol engines: £1,800-£4,200 supply only
M156 V8 (C63 AMG): £6,500-£15,000 for specialist rebuild
Labour adds £500-£1,000 for supply and fit service
items.cta: Get an instant Mercedes Benz C Class engine replacement quote

items.question: Is the OM651 timing chain failure as bad as everyone says?
items.answer: Yes - the OM651's rear-mounted timing chain is a genuine weak point. It typically stretches between 60,000-100,000 miles. If it snaps, the engine is usually beyond economic repair.
keyPoints: Listen for rattle on cold start - first warning sign
Preventative chain replacement costs £700-£1,100 at a specialist
Post-failure repair often exceeds £2,500 with head removal
Rebuilt OM651 units use upgraded timing components
items.cta: Compare rebuilt OM651 Mercedes C Class engine prices

items.question: Which Mercedes C Class engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM646 2.2-litre diesel (W203/W204) and M276 V6 petrol are widely considered the most reliable. Avoid early OM651 diesels and M274 petrols if you want trouble-free ownership.
keyPoints: OM646 2.2L diesel regularly exceeds 200,000 miles
M276 V6 petrol has no major systemic failures
M274 petrol suffers piston cracking (40k-80k miles)
OM651 pre-2013 has highest timing chain failure rate
items.cta: Find a reliable reconditioned engine for your Mercedes C Class

items.question: What causes the M274 piston cracking in the C300 and C200?
items.answer: A wrist pin seizure within the piston boss causes the piston skirt to crack - a design flaw in M274 engines. There is no preventative fix, and the first sign is often a misfire requiring full engine replacement.
keyPoints: NHTSA investigation opened due to failure frequency
No warning signs - failure appears suddenly
Specialist replacement: £3,200-£5,000 fitted
items.cta: Get quotes for M274 Mercedes C Class engine replacement

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine on an older Mercedes C Class?
items.answer: For a W203 or early W204 worth £2,000-£4,000, engine replacement usually isn't economical. For a W205 or facelift W204 worth £6,000+, a rebuilt engine with warranty often makes excellent sense.
keyPoints: W203 (2000-2007): replacement rarely justified unless car is sentimental
W204 (2007-2014): borderline - depends on condition and spec
W205 (2014-2021): almost always worth replacing with upgraded unit
Specialist replacement costs 40-50% less than main dealer
items.cta: Check if your Mercedes C Class qualifies for engine replacement

items.question: What are the common problems with the OM642 V6 diesel in the C350 CDI?
items.answer: The OM642's main weakness is oil cooler seal failure, typically at 80,000-120,000 miles. The job requires intake manifold removal, costing £800-£1,200 at a specialist. Ignoring it can destroy the engine.
keyPoints: Oil cooler seal leak is almost guaranteed by 100k miles
Early repair prevents oil starvation and turbo damage
Dealer price: £1,300-£1,900, specialist: £800-£1,200
Swirl flap failure also common on higher-mileage units
items.cta: Compare OM642 Mercedes C Class engine prices

items.question: Should I replace or rebuild my C Class engine?
items.answer: Replacement with a professionally rebuilt unit is usually cheaper and carries a better warranty than rebuilding your original engine. Only rebuild if the damage is minor or the car has sentimental value.
keyPoints: Specialist rebuild: £2,500-£5,000 with 12-month warranty typically
Replacement with recon unit: £1,800-£4,500 fitted, 12-24 month warranty
Dealer rebuild costs often exceed £6,000
Replacement is usually 2-3 day turnaround; rebuild takes 1-2 weeks
items.cta: Get Mercedes C Class engine rebuild vs replacement advice

items.question: What engine problems affect the W204 C63 AMG M156 V8?
items.answer: The pre-facelift M156 (engine numbers below #060658) suffers from defective head bolts that can snap, allowing coolant into cylinders. Preventative head bolt replacement is strongly recommended.
keyPoints: Head bolt failure typically occurs 30,000-60,000 miles
Preventative bolt replacement: £1,600-£2,400 at specialist
Post-failure repair costs £5,000+ and may not save the engine
Camshaft adjuster wear and lifter noise also common
items.cta: Find M156 replacement engines for Mercedes C63 AMG

items.question: Is the M264 engine in the W206 C Class reliable?
items.answer: The M264 is generally durable with many covering 200,000+ miles, but coolant leaks from plastic water pipes are common around 80,000 miles. It's a minor nuisance rather than a terminal failure.
keyPoints: Coolant pipe failures are the most reported issue
Replace with updated aluminium or improved plastic parts
No widespread timing chain or piston issues like older engines
48V mild hybrid system can fail but is usually repairable
items.cta: Compare Mercedes C Class M264 engine prices

items.question: What's the difference between reconditioned, rebuilt, and used C Class engines?
items.answer: Reconditioned means factory-spec with all new wear parts. Rebuilt means failed parts replaced. Used means removed from a donor car. Recon offers best warranty; used is cheapest but carries risk.
keyPoints: Reconditioned: £1,800-£4,500, 12-24 month warranty, better-than-new
Rebuilt: £1,400-£3,800, 12 month warranty, good value mid-option
Used: £800-£2,200, 30-90 day warranty typically, buyer beware
Supply & fit adds £500-£1,000 to any engine price
